title: Proverbs 24
subtitle: Do Not Envy Evil; Rescue the Staggering; The Sluggard's Field

sections.header: Verses 1–18 — Do Not Envy Evil; Rescue the Staggering; Wisdom Builds the House

heb.paragraph: Rescue (haṣṣēl) those being led away to death; hold back those staggering toward slaughter (v. 11). The imperative is urgent and unambiguous — there is no opt-out for bystanders. The verb nāṣal in the Hiphil means to snatch away, to physically intervene. The sage rejects the excuse 'we didn't know' (v. 12): God who weighs hearts knows what you knew.

Wisdom is like dĕbaš (honey) to the soul — sweet when you find it (v. 14). Honey was the sweetest substance in the ancient world and a sign of the promised land's bounty. The comparison is sensory: wisdom is not duty but delight, an acquired taste that becomes the most satisfying flavour in life.

panels.ctx: The first half of ch.24 contains the Thirty Sayings' wisdom-builds-the-house formula (v.3) and the obligation to rescue those staggering toward death (v.11). The honey-and-wisdom comparison (v.13-14) applies the sweetness metaphor: just as honey is good for the body, wisdom is good for the soul — with the same future-hope promise as 23:18.

cross.note: Wisdom has built her house — the personified Wisdom of ch.9 builds; wisdom in v.3 is the principle by which all houses are properly built.
Where there are no oxen the manger is clean, but abundant harvests come through the strength of an ox — the same diligence-requires-mess principle implicit in v.27.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rescue those led to death — the rhetorical question ('does not he who weighs the heart perceive it?') makes divine moral epistemology explicit: God evaluates what was known and withheld, not just what was done.

Do not envy evil men or desire to be with them — the chapter's answer to theodicy-envy is consistently temporal: the wicked have no future (v.20). Alter notes this is not denial of the present reality (the wicked do sometimes prosper) but insistence on the long view: the covenant framework evaluates outcomes over the full arc of a life, not at its peak of apparent success.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rescue those led to death; do not use ignorance as excuse — the God who weighs the heart knows what was known. The obligation to act for others in mortal danger cannot be discharged by selective ignorance.
Do not envy evil men — temporal reframe: the wicked have no future (v.20). Envy is stopped by seeing long-term reality, not by denying present appearances.
Calvin: 'Those who are able to rescue the innocent and do not, offering the excuse of ignorance, are not acquitted — God who weighs the heart knows what they knew.'

If you find wisdom, there is a future for you and your hope will not be cut off — wisdom is distinguished from all other earthly goods by the permanence of its benefit. Calvin notes that material prosperity can vanish overnight, reputation can be destroyed, health can fail. But wisdom, once genuinely acquired, cannot be taken away by any external circumstance.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: the rescue-instruction imposes a positive obligation to act, not merely permission. The disclaimer-rejection makes God the evaluator of what was known and decided.

The NET note on 'the wise have great power, and those with knowledge increase their strength' discusses the counter-intuitive claim: wisdom produces a kind of strength greater than physical power. The note traces the theme through Qohelet (Eccl 9:13-16: a poor wise man saved his city) and identifies wisdom as force-multiplier, not force-replacement.

sections.header: Verses 19–34 — Do Not Fret Over Evildoers; The Field of the Sluggard; Second Collection Begins

heb.paragraph: The field of the ʿāṣēl (sluggard) passage (vv. 30–34) is an eyewitness account: 'I went past the field of the sluggard … thorns had come up everywhere, the ground was covered with weeds, and the stone wall was in ruins.' The sage reads the landscape as a moral text — neglect is visible, and poverty comes like an armed man.

The wise observe and derive instruction — the process is called lāqaḥ mûsār (to take/receive discipline). The field of the sluggard is not merely a cautionary tale but a maśkîl moment: the discerning person can learn wisdom from negative examples. Every ruin tells a story if you have eyes to read it.

panels.ctx: The second half includes more sayings from the supplementary collection (vv.23-29) and the extended parable of the sluggard's field (vv.30-34) — one of the most vivid portraits of lazy neglect in Proverbs. The field that produced only weeds because its owner loved sleep is the book's sharpest illustration of what happens when diligence is absent.

cross.note: A little sleep, a little slumber — the sluggard passage of ch.6 is echoed and extended in vv.30-34.
If anyone is not willing to work, let them not eat — the NT application of Proverbs' consistent diligence ethic.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The honey-wisdom comparison — Alter notes the comparison is exact: present pleasure (taste) plus future benefit. Honey is genuinely pleasant; so is wisdom. Neither is merely obligatory.
The sluggard's field — unique in the proverb collections for its extended first-person observation. Wisdom as narrated personal experience, not just received tradition.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Honey and wisdom — as honey is sweet to the taste, wisdom is sweet to the soul and has a future/hope (v.14). Wisdom's sweetness is both present and future; folly's sweetness is only present.

The sluggard's field — one of Proverbs' most sustained observational wisdom passages. The teacher observes, reflects, and draws the conclusion. The method is empirical: wisdom can be read from the observable world.

Calvin: 'Though the righteous fall seven times, they rise again — the falling is not the mark of defeat; the rising is the mark of covenant faithfulness. The righteous person rises not because they never fail but because they have a foundation to rise from.'
Calvin: 'A little sleep, a little slumber — the moderation of the laziness makes the warning more pointed. It is not gross idleness but comfortable small indulgences that destroy.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: wisdom's future/hope (yesh acharit) — the distinctive temporal promise. Unlike all material goods, wisdom's benefits extend into the future without diminishment.
NET: verbatim repetition of 6:10-11 — the same excuse used repeatedly produces the same result. The literary echo signals that the sluggard's self-deception is chronic, not occasional.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"By wisdom a house is built, and through understanding it is established; through knowledge its rooms are filled with rare and beautiful treasures."NoteThe Proverbs account of how excellent human life is constructed. Not by brute force but by cultivated intelligence and relational knowledge.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: Rescue the Staggering
rows.text: Rescue those being led away to death; hold back those staggering toward slaughter. If you say I did not know about this -- does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? The positive obligation to act for others in mortal danger cannot be discharged by selective ignorance.

rows.label: The Sluggard's Field: Empirical Wisdom
rows.text: I went past the field of a sluggard... thorns had come up everywhere, the ground was covered with weeds. First-person observation, reflection, conclusion: wisdom can be read from the observable world.

tx.title: Additional 'Sayings of the Wise' — placement in LXX
tx.content: MT places a second 'Sayings of the Wise' collection at the end of chapter 24. In the LXX, this material appears in a completely different location — after chapter 30 (Agur) rather than in its MT position. This is one of the most dramatic structural differences in LXX Proverbs.
tx.note: The LXX's placement of the supplementary wisdom collection after Agur's sayings creates a different reading experience, grouping shorter collections together rather than interspersing them with Solomonic material.

debate.title: Proverbs' Empirical Method: Observation as a Source of Wisdom

positions.name: Valid source alongside revelation
positions.argument: The sluggard's-field observation exemplifies the empirical method: observe the world carefully and draw theological conclusions. Observation-based wisdom is a valid route to knowledge.

positions.name: Creation observation as general revelation
positions.argument: Wisdom read from creation expresses natural law embedded within it. God's wisdom is in the structure of things.
